query: is functional neurological disorder classified as a psychosomatic disorder

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Conversion disorder, also known as functional neurological symptom disorder (FND) , is a psychiatric disorder characterized by symptoms affecting sensory or motor function. These signs and symptoms are inconsistent with patterns of known neurologic diseases or other medical conditions. Although functional neurologic disorder has no organic basis, the symptoms significantly impact a patient’s ability to function. Moreover, the symptoms cannot be controlled at will and are not considered to be feigned intentionally by the patient. The term “conversion disorder” was first mentioned in literature by Sigmund Freud (1856-1939). The Austrian neurologist and founder of psychoanalysis believed that functional symptoms that could not be explained by neurologic diseases or other underlying medical conditions reflected an unconscious conflict. [1] In this context, the word "conversion" refers to replacing a somatic symptom with a repressed idea. The understanding of functional neurologic disorder is still largely limited and continually evolving. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Somatic symptom disorder – When the DSM-5 was released, the disorders previously referred to as somatoform disorders (including hypochondriasis and somatization separately) are now referred to as “somatic symptom disorder” collectively. The diagnostic criteria for somatic symptom disorder include one or more somatic symptoms that cause distress or lead to significant disruption of daily life, excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to these symptoms (which can present as mild, moderate, or severe in intensity), and persistence of symptoms (typically for greater than six months). There may be some overlap between somatic symptom disorder and functional neurologic disorder, as patients with functional neurologic disorder often display somatic symptoms that cause distress or disrupt daily life. However, patients with functional neurologic disorder typically do not have as exaggerated a response to their symptoms, and their symptoms tend to be inconsistent with true medical diagnoses.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of symptoms defines an acute episode of functional neurologic disorder for less than six months, and persistent functional neurologic disorder includes the presence of symptoms for greater than six months. Functional neurologic disorder can also have the specifier of with or without the psychological stressor. The DSM-5TR recognizes several subtypes of functional neurologic disorder, as listed below. There are many ways to differentiate between organic disorders and these subtypes of functional neurologic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Several psychiatric disorders are included in the differential diagnosis of functional neurologic disorder. Factitious disorder – In factitious disorder, patients exhibit signs and symptoms consistent with specific medical or psychiatric disorders, as occurs with functional neurologic disorder. However, patients with factitious disorder deliberately deceive physicians by exaggerating, faking, or lying about symptoms, hurting themselves to create symptoms, or altering their test results. The definition of this disorder is the patient having the desire to appear ill to obtain medical care but not to achieve personal or financial gain. There is also factitious disorder by proxy, in which a person acts as if someone under their care is sick. Patients with functional neurologic disorder do not intentionally create their symptoms to obtain care.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: One or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function. Clinical findings can provide evidence of incompatibility between the symptom and recognized neurological or medical conditions. Another medical or mental disorder does not better explain the symptom or deficit. The symptom or deficit results in clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other vital areas of functioning or warrants medical evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5TR edition (DSM-5TR) categorizes functional neurologic disorder as part of the “somatic symptom and related disorders.” In previous editions of the DSM, clear psychologic comorbidities were a required part of the diagnosis and the ability to show that the symptoms were not intentionally produced. [10] The DSM-5 has also removed “ la belle indifférence ” (a patient’s apparent indifference to their condition) as a diagnostic criterion. These are now considered features that support the diagnosis of functional neurologic disorder. Other supporting features include associated dissociative symptoms and recent psychological or physical trauma. The diagnostic criteria for functional neurologic disorder, according to the DSM-5TR, are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Functional neurologic disorder cannot truly be considered a diagnosis of exclusion, but diagnosis relies on the clinician to exclude major neurologic diseases and to look for inconsistencies on an exam, as well as clinical signs and symptoms that are not characteristic of organic disorders. Inconsistent symptoms, negative labs/imaging, and/or a significant psychiatric disorder are reliable indicators of functional neurologic disorder. However, the clinician should still exclude all other organic disorders with a thorough exam. Also, patients with functional neurologic disorder may have comorbid neurologic disorders, potentially making the diagnosis more difficult.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Illness anxiety disorder – Illness anxiety disorder was previously referred to as “hypochondriasis” in the DSM-4. Patients will illness anxiety disorder have very few somatic symptoms, if any. They present as having a preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness. These patients exhibit excessive health-related behaviors (e.g., repeatedly checking themselves for signs of disease) or exhibit maladaptive avoidance (eg, avoiding appointments with doctors or hospital visits). Patients with functional neurologic disorder do not typically display the level of preoccupation that patients with illness anxiety disorder do.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological, social, and biological factors can all contribute to, precipitate, or perpetuate functional neurologic disorder. Often, there is a trauma, adverse life event, or acute/chronic stressor preceding symptoms of functional neurologic disorder. Many patients with functional neurologic disorder are found to have a history of childhood abuse, both emotional and sexual. Other psychological factors contributing to functional neurologic disorder include poor coping skills and internal psychological conflicts. Patients with functional neurologic disorder are more likely to have certain psychiatric disorders (depression, anxiety, and personality disorders) than patients with known neurologic conditions. They are also more likely to have a history of multiple somatic complaints, including symptoms like generalized fatigue, weakness, or pain, without a known cause. [3] Physical injury or actual neurologic illness (such as a stroke or migraine) may “trigger” the symptoms of functional neurologic disorder. Less educated people, those of lower socioeconomic status, and patients living in developing or rural areas are more likely to develop functional neurologic disorder. There are two major models or hypotheses for functional neurologic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Psychodynamic models. These models suggest that somatic symptoms of functional neurologic disorder are a product of emotional conflict. This emotional conflict becomes repressed into the unconscious mind and converted into a symptom. The postulation is that this scenario is a type of defense mechanism against negative feelings that the emotional conflict would induce. Other psychodynamic models focus on the development of inadequate coping mechanisms and negative interpersonal relationships that develop earlier in a patient’s life. Later in life, these coping mechanisms or behaviors may recur if the patient encounters another traumatic event.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Malingering is not a psychiatric disorder but a behavior that patients may display. Malingering is a patient feigning or exaggerating symptoms of physical or psychiatric disorders for personal or financial gain. Specific examples include patients faking symptoms of illness to avoid a criminal conviction, avoiding obligations such as work, school, or their jobs, or obtaining addictive medications when they are not warranted. Unlike patients with factitious disorder, these patients are often not compliant with complete physical exams or treatment plans.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The primary tools used to diagnose functional neurologic disorder are the history and physical exam; however, laboratory studies or imaging may be required to rule out underlying medical conditions. The studies ordered are highly dependent on the nature of the patient’s symptoms and their initial presentation. For example, patients who present with psychogenic nonepileptic seizures may undergo an electroencephalogram to show a lack of true seizure activity, or they may have laboratory studies such as prolactin or creatine phosphokinase drawn to help differentiate their condition from an epileptic seizure. A vast majority of patients presenting with weakness as a primary symptom will undergo electromyography or magnetic resonance imaging tests. Studies may also be ordered for patients with possible comorbid diagnoses.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining an appropriate history is a crucial factor in functional neurologic disorder. Making the patient feel comfortable and encouraging them to share their symptoms and stories is crucial. One method to consider is creating a list of the patient’s symptoms at the initial visit; allow the patient to list all symptoms they have been experiencing recently. This initial step is essential so that one can document any subtle changes in symptoms throughout the illness and see if the patient lists multiple somatic symptoms. It is essential to ask the patient for a list of symptoms for which they have received treatment previously or suspected neurologic diagnoses. The clinician should focus on gathering details, such as the time frame of symptoms and context. Without revealing the suspected diagnosis, it is important to ask about recent events or stressors in the patient’s life and complete a psychiatric history. Family history should also include psychiatric components. The clinician may wait until the end of the interview to ask for psychiatric history. If the patient has experienced similar symptoms in the past, what was their diagnosis, and how were they treated? Patients with a history of functional neurologic disorder are more likely to have repeat episodes, even without a past diagnosis. These questions can also help shed light on the patient’s view of other providers and to show which specialists they have seen and what work-up they have had. Involving the patient is crucial to building rapport; ask them what they feel is happening. Instead of focusing on what the patient cannot do at the time of the interview, ask them to describe the last time they remember being symptom-free and ask them to describe what a typical day looks like for them.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Sternocleidomastoid test – Patients with functional neurologic disorder will often exhibit weakness when asked to rotate their head towards the affected side, whereas patients with the organic disease will not. Collapsing weakness – This phenomenon can be elicited by asking a patient to hold one of their limbs in a specific position; the examiner then applies light force to the limb, and it appears to “collapse” suddenly. Abnormal movement – Several psychogenic movement disorders are common in functional neurologic disorder, as listed below. Features typical of all psychogenic movement disorders include rapid onset, improvement with distracting tasks (such as counting or performing physical tasks simultaneously), and variability (in frequency, amplitude, or affected region of the body). As with paralysis and weakness, it is essential to carefully observe the patient in several settings to pick up these indicators.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Psychotherapy is the first-line treatment in most cases of functional neurologic disorder. Different types of therapy can be useful, but the most effective throughout the literature seems to be cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT). [11] CBT works by looking at how someone thinks and feels about a situation and how that influences their behavior. Predisposing factors can be analyzed, and patients should focus on improving their communication and ability to express emotions properly. Goldstein and his colleagues conducted one study highlighting the efficacy of CBT in treating functional neurologic disorder; although it used a small sample size, the study showed that CBT successfully reduced dissociative seizure frequency by at least 50%. [12] Other types of therapy that may prove helpful include psychodynamic psychotherapy (individualized and best for patients who have already accepted that their symptoms may be secondary to past trauma), group therapy (this may help patients learn from and support each other), and family therapy (this may help improve communication). Furthermore, hypnotherapy can ameliorate functioning and physical symptoms of functional neurologic disorder, even in particularly severe cases. These approaches seem to be effective, especially in the case of comorbidities, such as chronic pain conditions. [13] [14] However, hypnosis is considered a second-line treatment that may be helpful for patients with speech symptoms or sensory loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Somatic Symptom Disorder -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Somatic symptom disorder (SSD) is a recently defined diagnosis in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders , fifth edition (DSM-5). It is the manifestation of one or more physical symptoms accompanied by excessive thoughts, emotion, and/or behavior related to the symptom, which causes significant distress and/or dysfunction. [1] These symptoms may or may not be explained by a medical condition. The two major changes to the DSM-IV criteria included eliminating the requirement that somatic symptoms be organically unexplained and adding the requirement that certain psychobehavioral features have to be present to justify the diagnosis. The new criteria also eliminated somatization disorder, undifferentiated somatoform disorder, hypochondriasis, and pain disorder from the previous definitions. These revisions were intended to increase the relevance of SSD and its use in the primary care setting.

retrieved_docs.content: شقيقة, commonly known as migraine, is classified within multiple medical taxonomies that reflect its complex nature. It is recognized as a disorder of the brain and nerves, indicating its primary involvement of the central nervous system. The condition is also listed as a member of the Neurological Disorders subcluster in PsycINFO, underscoring its status as a neurological disease. In addition, شقيقة appears as a member of the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders subcluster in PsycINFO, highlighting the interplay between physical symptoms and psychosomatic factors in its presentation. The CELLOSAURUS Disease Terminology system includes شقيقة among its concepts, further confirming its acceptance as a distinct disease entity across standardized terminology resources. Together, these classifications illustrate that شقيقة spans neurological, physical, and psychosomatic domains, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and multidisciplinary management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine (شقيقة) is presented as a central neurological disorder that belongs to the broader category of nervous system and sense‑organ diseases, which in turn is a subcategory of pain (ألم). Within this hierarchy, migraine is linked to general headache classifications such as “Headache; including migraine” and to more specific entities like chronic mixed headache syndrome, which is a chronic nervous‑system disorder that has migraine as a child concept. Migraine is also recognized as a member of the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders subcluster, indicating its relevance to both somatic and psychological aspects of patient care. The condition is clinically similar to familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية), a hereditary nervous‑system disorder, and is related or possibly synonymous with vascular headache (صداع وعائي), reflecting overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1576
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders comprise a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. One such subtype, Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, is classified under the related category Anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition and is defined by explicit diagnostic criteria that are incorporated into the overall diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Another distinct entity, Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere, is recognized as a child category within the anxiety disorders hierarchy. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and the associated diagnostic criteria is essential for accurate identification and appropriate management of patients whose anxiety symptoms are linked to underlying medical or physiological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) and migraine (شقيقة) are both categorized under the broader domain of Brain and Nerves, indicating that they are considered sub‑entities within the nervous system specialty. Each condition is listed as a member of the PsycINFO subclusters Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) and Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), reflecting their relevance to both pure neurological pathology and psychosomatic aspects of disease. The CELLOSAURUS Disease Terminology explicitly includes migraine (شقيقة) as a concept, and the classification Migraine and Headache Syndromes designates migraine as a parent category, underscoring its status as a primary headache disorder. In clinical documentation, a history of migraine (H/O: migraine) is mapped to the personal anamnesis category “Persoonlijke anamnese met ziekten van zenuwstelsel en zintuigen” (personal history of diseases of the nervous system and senses) and is associated with the finding of migraine (شقيقة). Together, these relationships demonstrate that hydrocephalus and migraine are integrated within multiple medical classification systems, are linked to both neurological and psychosomatic domains, and are important considerations in patient history taking and diagnostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.content: The term شقيقة is recognized within the CELLOSAURUS Disease Terminology and is listed as a member of the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders subcluster in PsycINFO. شقيقة فالجية عائلية is classified as a hereditary disorder of the nervous system, indicating that this familial hemiplegic form of migraine is a genetically based subtype of شقيقة. It falls under the broader parent category Brain and Nerves and is included in the CELLOSAURUS Disease Terminology, reflecting its recognition in formal disease vocabularies. شقيقة is also a member of both the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) and the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), highlighting its relevance to both neurological and psychosomatic clinical domains. In the context of vascular pathology, شقيقة is identified as a manifestation of CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1, linking migraine symptoms to underlying cerebral arteriopathy and subcortical infarcts. The same arteriopathy also manifests اضطراب المِشْيَة (gait disorder), underscoring the multisystem impact of this genetic condition. شقيقة is clinically similar to شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial migraine) and is related and possibly synonymous with صداع وعائي (vascular headache), suggesting overlap in clinical presentation and pathophysiology between migraine, familial migraine, and vascular headache syndromes. Patient documentation such as H/O: migraine is mapped to personal anamnesis records concerning diseases of the nervous system and senses and lists شقيقة as an associated finding, demonstrating its importance in clinical history taking. Overall, شقيقة is interconnected with a range of neurological, vascular, genetic, and psychosomatic concepts, serving as a key entity in the understanding, classification, and management of headache disorders within diverse medical frameworks.
